subject: Chemistry
title: Robert Boyle: The End of Alchemy
description: A fast breakdown of Robert Boyle: The End of Alchemy for builders.

hook.text: In 1661, Robert Boyle published 'The Sceptical Chymist'—the book that killed alchemy and birthed modern chemistry.

buildup.text: Boyle rejected the four-element theory. He defined elements as substances that can't be broken down further.

discovery.text: He demanded that chemical knowledge come from experiments, not ancient texts or mystical authority.

twist.text: Despite writing the founding text of chemistry, Boyle himself still believed in transmutation and the philosopher's stone.

climax.text: His real contribution: chemistry should be empirical, reproducible, and public—no more secret codes.

punchline.text: He killed the old system while still believing parts of it. Science is messy.

quiz.question: What revolutionary idea did Robert Boyle introduce in 'The Sceptical Chymist'?
options: Elements are substances that cannot be broken down further
All matter is made of atoms
